title: South Korea AI and semiconductor mega-project faces a 2.3 GW power shortfall
gist: Wood Mackenzie warns South Korea's KRW 4,700 trillion (US$3.1 trillion) AI and semiconductor mega-project, including the Honam-region cluster, faces a ~2.3 GW power shortfall by 2030 as regional peak demand jumps from 11.8 GW to 19.1 GW against only 16.8 GW of effective capacity, making grid investment, BESS economics, market reform, and nuclear life extension the binding constraints on the AI buildout.

insights.claim: South Korea's Honam semiconductor and AI cluster alone could raise regional peak electricity demand from an estimated 11.8 GW today to 19.1 GW once fully developed, against forecast effective capacity of only 16.8 GW by 2030, leaving a ~2.3 GW shortfall.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: A hard 2.3 GW gap means power, not capital or chips, could throttle the world's largest planned AI/semiconductor hub.
insights.quote: leaving a potential shortfall of approximately 2.3 GW
insights.locator: According to Wood Mackenzie's new report

insights.claim: South Korea's flagship national mega-project is valued at KRW 4,700 trillion (US$3.1 trillion), including a planned KRW 800 trillion semiconductor and AI data centre cluster in the Honam region.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Scale of the bet underscores the systemic risk if power infrastructure lags the industrial timeline.
insights.quote: the country's flagship KRW 4,700 trillion (US$3.1 trillion) national mega-project

insights.claim: The constraint is framed as a sequencing challenge in which near-term flexibility measures, firm generation, and transmission upgrades must move in parallel or AI and semiconductor investment could become constrained by power infrastructure rather than technology or capital.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Power sequencing risk is the key variable to monitor for anyone exposed to Korean chip capex timelines.
insights.quote: If any one of those elements falls behind, the pace of AI and semiconductor investment could become constrained by power infrastructure rather than technology or capital.
insights.locator: This is ultimately a sequencing challenge

insights.claim: Honam has South Korea's strongest renewable energy potential, but the Honam-Central transmission corridor congests during spring months, preventing new solar and renewable projects from securing grid connections despite abundant resource.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Grid congestion, not resource scarcity, is the bottleneck, making transmission the highest-value fix.
insights.quote: The Honam-Central transmission corridor experiences congestion during spring months, preventing new renewable projects, including solar, from securing grid connections despite abundant resource availability.
insights.locator: Honam has the strongest renewable energy potential

insights.claim: Closing the gap through solar alone would need roughly 19.5 GW of new installations within five years, versus only 679 MW of solar added across the region in 2025, making a solar-only build-out unrealistic without rapid BESS expansion.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: The ~29x gap between required and actual solar build makes battery storage indispensable, not optional.
insights.quote: only 679 MW of solar capacity was added across the region during 2025
insights.locator: Closing the capacity gap through solar generation alone

insights.claim: Non-wired alternatives including dynamic line rating and transmission topology optimisation are highlighted as practical near-term measures to relieve grid congestion while larger transmission projects are developed.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Grid-enhancing technologies are a fast, capital-light lever ahead of multi-year transmission builds.
insights.locator: The report also highlights non-wired alternatives

insights.claim: Current market design is one of the largest barriers because batteries can presently earn revenue primarily through South Korea's central contract market, limiting commercial BESS deployment.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Without new revenue streams, private battery investment stalls; market reform is a prerequisite for the AI power buildout.
insights.quote: Batteries can presently generate revenue primarily through South Korea's central contract market, limiting commercial deployment.
insights.locator: Current market design remains one of the largest barriers

insights.claim: Either substantially expanding procurement volumes through the existing mechanism or introducing broader electricity market reforms that create additional revenue streams for BESS will be essential to support AI-driven power demand.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Watch Korean electricity market reform as the gating policy for battery investment upside.
insights.locator: Wood Mackenzie finds that either substantially expanding

insights.claim: Extending the operating life of the Hanbit Nuclear Power Plant is the fastest source of additional firm capacity, as a 10-year life extension could preserve about 2 GW that would otherwise retire, with around 3 GW of Hanbit's 5.9 GW fleet scheduled to reach end-of-life by 2035.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Nuclear life extension is the cheapest, fastest firm-capacity lever; Hanbit's retirement schedule is a key catalyst.
insights.quote: A 10-year life extension could preserve approximately 2 GW of generation that would otherwise retire
insights.locator: For reliable generation, the report identifies extending

insights.claim: Natural gas remains a viable near-term option, but global gas turbine supply chains have lead times of five to six years, so prioritising domestically manufactured turbines could deliver capacity under South Korea's forthcoming 12th Basic Plan for Electricity Supply and Demand.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Five-to-six-year turbine lead times make domestic turbine manufacturing a strategic advantage for hitting the timeline.
insights.quote: global gas turbine supply chains continue to experience lead times of five to six years
insights.locator: Natural gas also remains a viable near-term option

insights.claim: Reserved land originally intended for Hanbit Units 7 and 8 offers brownfield nuclear development that could cut construction costs by roughly 10-15% and shorten timelines versus greenfield.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Brownfield nuclear sites are a lower-cost structural solution to Honam's long-term demand.
insights.quote: which could reduce construction costs by approximately 10–15% while shortening project timelines compared with greenfield development
insights.locator: Reserved land originally intended for Hanbit Units 7 and 8

insights.claim: Reinforcing the Yeongnam-Honam transmission corridor would let surplus nuclear generation from Yeongnam, where effective capacity exceeds regional peak demand by 15.9 GW, support Honam's growing industrial load.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: Yeongnam's 15.9 GW surplus is a ready supply pool if inter-regional transmission is reinforced.
insights.quote: where effective capacity exceeds regional peak demand by 15.9 GW

insights.claim: As new industrial demand increasingly consumes renewable generation within Honam itself, less electricity will need to flow north, potentially reducing the scale required for the proposed 8 GW West Coast HVDC transmission project.
insights.speaker: Wood Mackenzie
insights.so_what: AI load co-located with renewables could shrink planned HVDC transmission needs, altering national grid capex plans.
insights.quote: potentially reducing the scale required for the proposed 8 GW West Coast HVDC transmission project
insights.locator: As new industrial demand increasingly consumes renewable generation
